g.data-narration: Welcome to Binary Logic, the foundation of all digital systems from microcontrollers to supercomputers.
g.text: Binary Logic: The Foundation of Digital Systems

g.data-narration: Binary logic uses only two states, 0 and 1, to represent information in all digital circuits and computers.
Binary variables have four equivalent representations: 0 or 1, LOW or HIGH, FALSE or TRUE, OFF or ON.
g.text: Binary Variables Can Be Represented As

g.data-narration: The NOT operation inverts its single input: if A is 0, output F is 1, and vice versa.
g.text: NOT Operation (Inverter) - F = Ā or F = A'

g.data-narration: The AND operation outputs 1 only when ALL inputs are 1. If any input is 0, output is 0.
g.text: AND Operation - F = A · B or F = AB

g.data-narration: The OR operation outputs 1 if ANY input is 1. Only outputs 0 when ALL inputs are 0.
g.text: OR Operation - F = A + B (Logical Addition)

g.data-narration: NAND is NOT-AND, the complement of AND. It outputs 0 only when all inputs are 1, otherwise outputs 1.
g.text: Universal gate - can build any circuit

g.data-narration: NOR is NOT-OR, the complement of OR. It outputs 1 only when all inputs are 0, otherwise outputs 0.
XOR outputs 1 only when inputs are DIFFERENT. It's crucial for binary addition and parity checking.
g.text: XOR Gate (Exclusive-OR) - F = A ⊕ B = ĀB + AB̄

g.data-narration: XNOR outputs 1 only when inputs are the SAME. It's the complement of XOR, useful for equality detection.
g.text: XNOR Gate (Exclusive-NOR) - F = (A ⊕ B)' = AB + ĀB̄

g.data-narration: Truth tables systematically list all input combinations and their outputs. For n inputs, there are 2^n rows.
g.text: A truth table systematically lists ALL possible input combinations and their corresponding outputs.
Number of rows = 2^n where n = number of inputs

g.data-narration: Master the three primitives AND, OR, NOT and their complements NAND, NOR, XOR, XNOR to build complex circuits.
Binary logic is essential for combinational and sequential circuits, forming the core of computer organization and design.
g.text: Mastery of binary logic is non-negotiable for hardware engineering and processor design

g.data-narration: These fundamentals connect to combinational circuits, ALUs, memory, and ultimately complete processor design.
